lineage.summary: Evidence-separated lineage for the bounded U-M selection of Heuser's title page, seventeen-poem main text, and scholarly appendix. The graph distinguishes the pinned repository XML, conceptual/current U-M item, exact raw U-M XML member, raw compound carrier, 1904 scholarly edition, historical intermediary editions, nineteen manuscript carriers, modern support expressions, and provider-specific facsimile, access, and rights layers. Direct and indirect witness routes remain explicit, and the disputed Harley 1706 attribution is retained only as tentative.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous medieval authors, except Friar Michael Kildare for the first poem only
catalogSummary.date: Medieval texts in manuscripts chiefly of the fourteenth to sixteenth centuries; scholarly edition 1904; electronic selection 2006
catalogSummary.region: South-east Ireland, probably Waterford, for the principal Harley MS 913 witness; appendix sources are multi-regional
catalogSummary.title: Die Kildare-Gedichte (U-M main-text-and-appendix selection)

catalogingSubject.label: Die Kildare-Gedichte (U-M main-text-and-appendix selection)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The exact bounded electronic selection represented by the pinned XML: Heuser's title page, the seventeen-component main text on printed pages 77-184, and the scholarly appendix on pages 185-229. It is not one medieval work, an authorial collection, the complete 1904 book, the complete contents of Harley MS 913, or a set of seventeen additional appendix works.

contentParts.label: Die Kildare-Gedichte U-M selection
notes: Hierarchy wrapper; all metrics are deliberately zero.
contentParts.summary: Root wrapper for the title page, main text, and appendix selected for electronic encoding.

contentParts.label: Title page
notes: Hierarchy wrapper; all metrics are deliberately zero.
contentParts.summary: Front of the Heuser title page, encoded from U-M image 00000001.

contentParts.label: Main text
notes: Hierarchy wrapper; all metrics are deliberately zero.
contentParts.summary: Heuser's seventeen-component edition of the Kildare poems on printed pages 77-184.

contentParts.label: I. Hymn by Michael Kildare
contentParts.summary: A 150-line hymn whose closing names Frere Michel Kyldare.

contentParts.label: Sarmun
contentParts.summary: The first of four separately headed sermon poems inside Heuser's Roman-numeral unit II.

contentParts.label: Quindecim Signa ante Judicium
contentParts.summary: A fragment of the Fifteen Signs before Judgment; editorial argument quotations are excluded.

contentParts.label: Fall and Passion
contentParts.summary: A religious poem on the Fall and Passion.

contentParts.label: Ten Commandments
contentParts.summary: The fourth separately headed sermon poem in Heuser's unit II.

contentParts.label: Seven Sins
contentParts.summary: One fragmentary poem across two XML sections and discontiguous Harley loci.

contentParts.label: Christ on the Cross
contentParts.summary: A fragment treated by Heuser as one poem across two cataloged incipits.

contentParts.label: A Song on the Times
contentParts.summary: A political and social poem across discontiguous Harley loci.

contentParts.label: Nego
contentParts.summary: A short main-text verse component.

contentParts.label: The Land of Cokaygne
contentParts.summary: The Land of Cokaygne, excluding seven verse lines quoted in editorial argument.

contentParts.label: Satire
contentParts.summary: Heuser's Satire on the people of Kildare; the editorial parenthetical does not prove the unnamed town is Kildare.

contentParts.label: Pers of Birmingham
contentParts.summary: A satirical main-text poem.

contentParts.label: A Rhyme-beginning Fragment
contentParts.summary: A twelve-line fragment also known by the modern title Repentance of Love.

contentParts.label: Elde

locators.value: f. 54v and f. 62r; IIIF canvases 116 and 131

contentParts.summary: A mortality poem across two Harley leaves.

contentParts.label: A Lullaby
contentParts.summary: The thirty-six-line Middle English base poem; its Latin translation stream is accounted separately.

contentParts.label: Lullaby Latin translation stream
notes: Descriptive child wrapper; metrics are zero to prevent double counting.
contentParts.summary: Twelve Latin lines in two groups, structurally nested here but counted only in part:parallel.

contentParts.label: Erthe
contentParts.summary: The forty-two-line Middle English base poem; interleaved Latin lines are accounted separately without duplicating the seven bilingual verse groups.

contentParts.label: Erthe parallel Latin stream
notes: Descriptive child wrapper; metrics are zero to prevent double counting.
contentParts.summary: Forty-two interleaved Latin lines inside the seven base-poem verse groups, counted only in part:parallel.

contentParts.label: Five Evil Things
contentParts.summary: The distinct seven-line Harley poem; Cotton and Rawlinson analogues are descriptive children and part of the separate parallel accounting stream.

contentParts.label: Cotton Cleopatra C VI analogue
notes: Descriptive child wrapper; the seven lines and one group are counted only in part:parallel.
contentParts.summary: Seven-line comparison version attributed by Heuser to Cotton Cleopatra C VI f. 22.

contentParts.label: Rawlinson poet. 32 analogue
notes: Descriptive child wrapper; the six lines and one group are counted only in part:parallel.
contentParts.summary: Six-line analogue mediated through Wright and Halliwell's Reliquiae Antiquae.

contentParts.label: Parallel translations and analogue versions
notes: Twelve Lullaby Latin lines in two groups, forty-two Erthe Latin lines in zero additional groups because they are interleaved in seven base groups, and seven Cotton plus six Rawlinson lines in two groups.
contentParts.summary: One mutually exclusive accounting stream for the nested Lullaby and Erthe Latin lines and the Cotton and Rawlinson analogues.

contentParts.label: Scholarly appendix
notes: Hierarchy wrapper; all metrics are deliberately zero.
contentParts.summary: Versions of selected poems and previously unused Anglo-Irish sources on printed pages 185-229.

contentParts.label: Appendix target-text accounting stream
notes: All 1,208 appendix L elements and 66 LG elements count as serialized target-text extent but create no additional intellectual item. Four prose extracts likewise create no prose-only item.
contentParts.summary: One mutually exclusive zero-item apparatus record for all appendix verse and prose source publications.

contentParts.label: Appendix part 1: versions of selected poems
notes: Descriptive wrapper; its 1,001 verse lines are counted only in part:appendix-accounting.
contentParts.summary: Eleven scoped witness-version publications on printed pages 185-215.

contentParts.label: Commandments and Seven Sins versions
notes: Descriptive wrapper; all metrics are zero.
contentParts.summary: Selected versions from Laud Misc. 463, disputed Harley 1706, Arundel 20, and Laud Misc. 416.

contentParts.label: Christ-on-the-Cross analogues
notes: Descriptive wrapper; all metrics are zero. The Durham route is indirect through Furnivall.
contentParts.summary: Six selected analogues from New College 88, Bodl. 42, Durham A.III.12 through Furnivall, Arundel 507, and Add 31042.

contentParts.label: Lullay
notes: Descriptive wrapper; all metrics are zero.
contentParts.summary: Thirty-six-line Harley 7358 version at f. 12v.

contentParts.label: Earth
notes: Descriptive wrapper; all metrics are zero.
contentParts.summary: Eighty-two-line Cambridge University Library Ii.4.9 version.

contentParts.label: Appendix part 2: previously unused Anglo-Irish sources
notes: Descriptive wrapper; all metrics are zero.
contentParts.summary: Four prose extracts and five Douce 104 verse extracts on printed pages 215-229.

contentParts.label: Four prose source extracts
notes: Complete source publications inside the appendix, but zero newly cataloged prose works.
contentParts.summary: Conquest of Ireland and Secreta Secretorum excerpts from Lambeth 598, 633, and 623 and Bodleian Laud Misc. 526.

contentParts.label: Douce 104 extracts
notes: Descriptive wrapper; all 207 verse lines are counted only in part:appendix-accounting.
contentParts.summary: Tutivillus and four Piers Plowman C-text extracts from one physical manuscript witness.

dateStatements.display: Heuser scholarly edition published in Bonn in 1904
notes: This dates the edited selection and apparatus, not the composition of all medieval components.

dateStatements.display: Principal Harley MS 913 witness copied circa 1330-1370

scope.description: Date of the principal witness, not a uniform composition date

extent.description: Nineteen mutually exclusive manifest accounting records: seventeen main poem records plus one parallel/version stream and one appendix-apparatus stream. The 17 poems are all verse-only. Direct target-text extent is 3,203 lines in 377 verse groups: 1,928 base-poem lines, 67 parallel/analogue lines, and 1,208 appendix lines. Eighty-nine editorial quotation lines are excluded. Four appendix prose extracts remain zero-item apparatus. Raw XML structure has 78 DIV1-DIV5 elements and is not represented by the sections value.

formStatements.description: An edited selection combining verse, four appendix prose extracts, and scholarly apparatus; this display form is separate from the count-bearing item-form axis.
All seventeen count-bearing main components are verse-only.
Zero-item scholarly apparatus containing verse parallels and four prose source extracts.

scope.description: Principal medieval textual language across the main poems and many appendix extracts

languageStatements.label: German
notes: The XML header's legacy ger code captures only this layer and is incomplete as a description of the selection.

scope.description: Heuser's headings, arguments, notes, and scholarly apparatus
Parallel translations, rubrics, quotations, and source material
Old French comparison lines quoted in the editorial argument to Quindecim Signa ante Judicium; these editorial quotations are outside the 3,203 direct target-text verse-line accounting

notes: The conventional sixteen-poem count omits the seven-line Five Evil Things at Harley MS 913 f. 6v; Heuser/U-M structure, the BL contents record, UCC's modern edition, and Sinisi's explicit explanation support seventeen components for this electronic selection.

The content hierarchy is intentionally richer than the nineteen-record accounting axis. Every wrapper and descriptive child has zero item and verse metrics, so only the seventeen main records, part:parallel, and part:appendix-accounting contribute to extent totals.
Appendix completeness does not create new cataloged works: its versions, source publications, and four prose extracts remain scholarly apparatus within this bounded edited selection.

next_steps: Ask British Library staff to reconcile Heuser's paper, fifteenth-century, fol. 306 claim with current Harley 1706 records.
Inspect catalog history and the manuscript; do not silently substitute Harley 5396.
openQuestions.question: What manuscript and locus underlie Heuser's Harley 1706 appendix attribution at fol. 306, given the current 216-folio parchment description and nonmatching contents?

next_steps: Consult recent linguistic, codicological, and literary scholarship component by component.
Keep witness origin, composition place, conventional Kildare labeling, and named authorship separate.
openQuestions.question: How should component-specific composition places and anonymous authorship be refined without generalizing the probable Waterford origin of Harley MS 913 or Michael Kildare's single explicit attribution?

scope.description: Origin of Harley MS 913, not necessarily composition place of every poem
Probable origin of the principal witness; Kildare remains a conventional collection label
The 1904 edited main text and scholarly appendix represented by the electronic selection

notes: The explicit closing does not license a blanket attribution of Harley MS 913 or the edited selection.

scope.description: I. Hymn by Michael Kildare only
Uncorrected OCR and structural encoding of the title page, main text, and appendix selected for U-M

subjects.label: Medieval Ireland and Hiberno-English textual culture
Textual transmission, variants, and philological editing

summaries.value: U-M deliberately encoded the title page, Heuser's main text on pages 77-184, and the appendix on pages 185-229, omitting the rest of the 1904 volume. The main text contains seventeen count-once poems; the appendix and nested parallel/version streams remain zero-item scholarly apparatus.

The principal Harley MS 913 poems range across devotional, homiletic, moral, satirical, political, lyric, and mortality verse. The appendix publishes parallel versions, analogues, four prose extracts, Tutivillus, and Piers Plowman C-text passages from sixteen physical appendix witnesses.

The extent uses nineteen mutually exclusive accounting records: seventeen main poem records, one parallel/version record, and one appendix-apparatus record. Structural wrappers and descriptive child parts carry zero metrics. The raw XML contains seventy-eight DIV1-DIV5 elements, so nineteen is not a raw XML division count.

notes: The qualifier makes the electronic boundary explicit without pretending that the XML transcribes the complete 1904 volume.
